title: Getting Started
text: Presentation class includes several helpful fields methods. likely interact : Presentations pretty unique objects behave differently objects used . presentation objects R6 objects use reference semantics. means object copied modification. words, move, modify, copy r2slides::presentation object, change reflected everywhere object bound. reference semantics enable pretty cool behavior. may noticed new_presentation() register_presentation() argument called set_active. TRUE (default) presentation object set active presentation. means part don’t specify presentation anything, automatically grab presentation object. can always check presentation active calling presentation$is_active print(presentation). Alternatively, can call get_active_presentation() retrieve current active presentation object.
code: presentation$title # Returns the title of the presentation presentation$last_refreshed # Returns the last time the presentation was refreshed from Google Drive presentation$browse() # Opens the presentation in a browser presentation$refresh() # Refreshes the presentation from Google Drive to sync changes presentation$get_url() # Returns the URL of the presentation presentation$copy() # Creates a copy of the presentation in Google Drive  print(presentation) # Prints a summary of the presentation

title: Getting Started
text: Slides backbones r2slides. core, slide object contains reference presentation object unique identifier points correct slide. variety slide selection helpers can used create slide objects. can pass presentation object functions (working multiple presentations R session), editing one presentations time, can omit presentation argument default active presentation. Slide objects couple goodies might find helpful. can use == operator compare slides. return TRUE two slides duplicated (, every item slide exactly equivalent). can also use print() function print summary slide. addition, slide objects fields calculated access . mainly used internally intended direct use.
code: on_slide_url("<URL GOES HERE>") # Creates a slide object from a URL on_slide_id("slide_id") # Creates a slide object from a slide ID on_slide_number(2) # Creates a slide object that references the 2nd slide in a presentation on_slide_after(slide) # Creates a slide object that references the slide after the reference slide on_slide_after(slide, offset = -1) # Creates a slide object that references the slide before the reference slide. Offset in the number of slides after the reference slide.

title: Positioning System
text: building presentations programmatically, need robust system controlling objects appear slides. r2slides package provides exactly positioning system, treats every visual element—whether text, charts, shapes—geometric object explicit coordinates dimensions. core abstraction slide_position object captures location (top left coordinates), size (width height), rotation angle, slide dimensions. measurements expressed inches, keeps system intuitive aligned designers typically think slide layouts. positioning system designed around several key principles: positions explicit geometric objects, transformations functional composable, layout logic can abstracted reused. Instead scattering numeric coordinates throughout slide-building code, define positions reuse transform declaratively, enabling scalable slide generation across many datasets presentations.

title: Positioning System
text: create positions using slide_position() function, takes coordinates dimensions arguments. default, slides assumed 10 × 5.625 inches, matching Google Slides’ default dimensions, though can specify different slide sizes needed.1 creates rectangle positioned 1 inch top slide 2 inches left edge, dimensions 4 inches wide 2 inches tall. coordinate system follows standard conventions: positions defined relative top-left corner slide, top increasing downward left increasing right. need visualize position looks like development, slide_position objects implement plot() method makes easy inspect layouts without rendering full slide:  visual feedback can faster actually putting elements location slide. thicker line indicates side considered ‘top’ position, becomes important start applying rotations.
code: pos <- slide_position(   top = 1,   left = 2,   width = 4,   height = 2 ) plot(pos)

title: Positioning System
text: Rotation adds another dimension positioning. specify rotation angle degrees, object rotates clockwise around center point. Critically, rotation doesn’t change object’s intrinsic width height—instead, ’s applied affine transformation. distinction intrinsic dimensions rotated footprint becomes important advanced layouts. positioning system handles rotation correctly computed affine transform components (scaleX, scaleY, shearX, shearY), ensuring rotated objects render properly. want understand un-rotated bounding boxes, bounding_box() function calculates smallest axis-aligned rectangle contains one rotated objects. r2slides provides convenient helpers common positions. Functions like in_top_left(), in_top_middle(), in_bottom_right() return pre-configured slide_position objects sized appropriately typical content placement. really nothing special functions positioning, just defaults.
code: rotated <- slide_position(   top = 1,   left = 1,   width = 3,   height = 1.5,   rotation = 30 )

title: Positioning System
text: positioning system truly shines. Static coordinates work fine one-slides, programmatic slide generation demands flexibility. rarely want objects exactly position every time—instead, want express layout intent: “place annotation slightly chart” “position label right element.” relative_position() function enables exactly kind declarative positioning. takes existing position applies transformations create new position, leaving original unchanged. dimension—top, left, width, height—can transformed independently using either function scalar numeric value. pass function transformation, receives original value returns new value. pass scalar numeric, replaces original value entirely. dual interface handles relative adjustments (“move right half inch”) absolute replacements (“set width exactly 3 inches”). real power emerges combine relative positioning fact always returns new slide_position object. functional approach means can chain transformations, compose programmatically, reuse positioning logic without side effects hidden state.
code: base <- slide_position(1, 1, 4, 2)  # Move the object right by 0.5 inches moved <- relative_position(   base,   left_transformation = \(x) x + 0.5 )  # Set an absolute top position absolute_top <- relative_position(   base,   top_transformation = 2 )

title: Positioning System
text: real-world slide generation workflows, rarely apply transformations just . layout patterns repeat: annotations always appear charts specific way, delta indicators positioned consistently relative base elements, titles maintain standard spacing relationships. Writing relative_position() calls repeatedly quickly become tedious error-prone. Instead, define_relative_transformation_function() lets capture transformation logic reusable functions. creates function takes slide_position returns transformed version. can think version relative_position, transformations pre-programmed. pattern scales beautifully complex reporting workflows. Consider situation ’re generating slides several charts annotations chart. define relative transformation functions put month month change year year change relative position chart. Notice functions encode layout intent rather hard-coded coordinates. move /left/right certain amount. need adjust spacing across slides, change values just month2month year2year definitions reuse code. approach transforms slide generation manually positioning element defining transformation rules express relationships. code stops saying “put text coordinates (2.3, 4.7)” starts saying “place monthly change indicators 0.13 inches right 0.5 inches top left chart standard delta dimensions.” latter maintainable semantically meaningful.
code: annotation_transform <- define_relative_transformation_function(   top_transformation = \(x) x - 0.5,   width_transformation = 0.5,   height_transformation = 0.25 ) add_text("Label", in_top_left() |> annotation_transform()) month2month <- define_relative_transformation_function(   top_transformation = \(x) x -0.5,   left_transformation = \(x) x + 0.13,   width_transformation = 0.75,   height_transformation = 0.25 )  year2year <- define_relative_transformation_function(   top_transformation = \(x) x -0.3,   left_transformation = \(x) x + 0.13,   width_transformation = 0.75,   height_transformation = 0.25 )

title: Positioning System
text: combination base positions, relative transformations, reusable transformation functions creates complete layout system. ’s works practice: First, establish base positions using explicit coordinates. define transformation functions encode layout rules—spatial relationships different element types. Finally, apply transformations adding content slides. base chart position changes—perhaps need accommodate different slide template adjust spacing—derived elements move automatically. ’re hunting code update dozens coordinate pairs; ’re changing one base position letting transformation functions handle rest. pattern extends beyond simple offsets. Transformation functions can implement complex logic implementing maximum width annotations, mirroring positions right--left layouts, adjusting rotation. key layout logic centralized transformation functions can reused adjusted independently content-adding code. separation concerns leads code ’s easier read, maintain, scale across large presentations.
code: chart_pos <- in_top_left()  # Add the chart # Add annotations using transformation functions add_linked_chart(..., chart_pos) |>   add_text(   "Month-over-month change",   chart_pos |> month2month() ) |>   add_text(   "Year-over-year change",   chart_pos |> year2year() )

title: Text Styling and Style Rules
text: Text slides rarely just text. carries emphasis, hierarchy, direction, statistical meaning, brand identity. r2slides, text styling built around two composable ideas: text style defines formatting properties. style rule decides apply styles. declarative design provides powerful flexable way control appearance text slides. describe text look like certain conditions, r2slides handles implementation call add_text() add_text_multi(). approach taken r2slides separate visual definitions logic apply makes styling code maintainable resusable.

title: Text Styling and Style Rules
text: text style created text_style(). defines text appearance properties apply passed add_text() add_text_multi(). argument corresponds visual property. Colors currently specified RGB vectors (values 0 1)1. Font size points. defines reusable style object can applied text add_text() add_text_multi(). main idea styles objects defined reused consistently across slides. Text styles can combined using +. allows define base style extend controlled ways. highllight_label style inherits everything base_label style, bold style. matter add already completed styling objects together, simply specify additional styles text_style(). + operator, override styles, add non-conflicting properties. encourages composable approach styling build styles smaller pieces.2 understand text_style(), next question : apply styles conditionally?
code: title_style <- text_style(   font_family = "Roboto",   font_size   = 24,   bold        = TRUE,   text_color  = c(0, 0, 0) ) base_label <- text_style(   font_family = "Roboto",   font_size   = 12 )  bold <- text_style(bold = TRUE)  highlight_label <- base_label +   bold +   text_style(     text_color = c(0.8, 0.2, 0.2)   )

title: Text Styling and Style Rules
text: Text styles can combined using +. allows define base style extend controlled ways. highllight_label style inherits everything base_label style, bold style. matter add already completed styling objects together, simply specify additional styles text_style(). + operator, override styles, add non-conflicting properties. encourages composable approach styling build styles smaller pieces.2 understand text_style(), next question : apply styles conditionally?
code: base_label <- text_style(   font_family = "Roboto",   font_size   = 12 )  bold <- text_style(bold = TRUE)  highlight_label <- base_label +   bold +   text_style(     text_color = c(0.8, 0.2, 0.2)   )

title: Text Styling and Style Rules
text: text styles define text look like, style rules define apply styles. style_rule() function creates conditional styling logic. style rule answers two questions: style apply? , part text? argument contains one selection functions. argument contains corresponding text styles. argument takes list functions return either: TRUE/FALSE apply style entire text c(START_INDEX, END_INDEX) apply style specific substring Let’s begin simple example. Suppose want positive numbers appear green negative numbers red. selection functions return TRUE FALSE. TRUE, style applies entire string. Style rules can apply styles specific parts text returning index ranges: apply different styling objects different parts text. first selection function finds substring “±” applies bold, larger font style. second selection function finds substring including “±” applies smaller, non-bold style. r2slides three built-string selection helpers make easy target specific parts text: str_before() str_after() str_matches() functions return start end indices substring matches condition. can specify whether return boundary character part selection, , choose behavior character found. can also write custom selection functions target text complex ways. built-helpers aren’t sufficient, can write selector. Selection functions run inside special data mask, can customize values access , confident incorrectly picking something global environment. data mask provides access text (full text string) whatever variables pass … argument add_text() add_text_multi(). Let’s try bit complex example based positive/negative example earlier. Suppose want style positive negative numbers differently, also statistically significant. can write custom selection function checks text positive negative whether result significant: rule : Apply blue text ▲ found stat_sig TRUE Apply orange text ▼ found stat_sig TRUE Apply black text stat_sig FALSE can see, selection functions access text stat_sig variable passed . allows us write complex conditional styling logic depends content text external data. limit amount additional data can pass - need named. also limit complexity number selection functions. can use R code want inside , run clean environment access text variables pass .
code: positive_style <- text_style(text_color = c(0.1, 0.6, 0.2)) negative_style <- text_style(text_color = c(0.8, 0.2, 0.2))  pos_neg_rule <- style_rule(   when = c(     \() value >= 0,     \() value < 0   ),   what = c(     positive_style,     negative_style   ) ) estimate_style_rule <- style_rule(   when = list(     \() str_before(text, '±'),  # Returns c(start, end) indices     \() str_after(text, '±', include_boundary = TRUE)   ),   what = list(     text_style(bold = TRUE, font_size = 14),     text_style(bold = FALSE, font_size = 10)   ) ) # Base style base_stat_sig_style <- text_style(font_size = 4, font_family = 'Roboto', bold = TRUE)  # Style for stat sig increase change_stat_sig_increase <- base_stat_sig_style +    text_style(text_color = c(0.29, 0.53, 0.91))  # Style for stat sig decrease change_stat_sig_decrease <- base_stat_sig_style +    text_style(text_color = c(0.902, 0.569, 0.220))  # Style for no statistical significance change_no_stat_sig <- base_stat_sig_style +    text_style(text_color = c(0, 0, 0))   # Our styling rule object change_style <- style_rule(    when = c(      \() stat_sig == TRUE & str_detect(text, '▲'),      \() stat_sig == TRUE & str_detect(text, '▼'),      \() stat_sig == FALSE ),    what = c(      change_stat_sig_increase,      change_stat_sig_decrease,      change_no_stat_sig))   add_text(slide_obj = on_slide_number(2),          text = "▲5.4%", # Text to style          position = slide_position_3, # Where to put it          text_style = change_style, # How to style it          stat_sig = TRUE) # Any additional data we want our selection functions to have access to

what: Conditional Styling to the Whole Text
title: Text Styling and Style Rules
text: Let’s begin simple example. Suppose want positive numbers appear green negative numbers red. selection functions return TRUE FALSE. TRUE, style applies entire string.
code: positive_style <- text_style(text_color = c(0.1, 0.6, 0.2)) negative_style <- text_style(text_color = c(0.8, 0.2, 0.2))  pos_neg_rule <- style_rule(   when = c(     \() value >= 0,     \() value < 0   ),   what = c(     positive_style,     negative_style   ) )

title: Text Styling and Style Rules
text: Style rules can apply styles specific parts text returning index ranges: apply different styling objects different parts text. first selection function finds substring “±” applies bold, larger font style. second selection function finds substring including “±” applies smaller, non-bold style. r2slides three built-string selection helpers make easy target specific parts text: str_before() str_after() str_matches() functions return start end indices substring matches condition. can specify whether return boundary character part selection, , choose behavior character found. can also write custom selection functions target text complex ways.
code: estimate_style_rule <- style_rule(   when = list(     \() str_before(text, '±'),  # Returns c(start, end) indices     \() str_after(text, '±', include_boundary = TRUE)   ),   what = list(     text_style(bold = TRUE, font_size = 14),     text_style(bold = FALSE, font_size = 10)   ) )

text: built-helpers aren’t sufficient, can write selector. Selection functions run inside special data mask, can customize values access , confident incorrectly picking something global environment. data mask provides access text (full text string) whatever variables pass … argument add_text() add_text_multi(). Let’s try bit complex example based positive/negative example earlier. Suppose want style positive negative numbers differently, also statistically significant. can write custom selection function checks text positive negative whether result significant: rule : Apply blue text ▲ found stat_sig TRUE Apply orange text ▼ found stat_sig TRUE Apply black text stat_sig FALSE can see, selection functions access text stat_sig variable passed . allows us write complex conditional styling logic depends content text external data. limit amount additional data can pass - need named. also limit complexity number selection functions. can use R code want inside , run clean environment access text variables pass .
code: # Base style base_stat_sig_style <- text_style(font_size = 4, font_family = 'Roboto', bold = TRUE)  # Style for stat sig increase change_stat_sig_increase <- base_stat_sig_style +    text_style(text_color = c(0.29, 0.53, 0.91))  # Style for stat sig decrease change_stat_sig_decrease <- base_stat_sig_style +    text_style(text_color = c(0.902, 0.569, 0.220))  # Style for no statistical significance change_no_stat_sig <- base_stat_sig_style +    text_style(text_color = c(0, 0, 0))   # Our styling rule object change_style <- style_rule(    when = c(      \() stat_sig == TRUE & str_detect(text, '▲'),      \() stat_sig == TRUE & str_detect(text, '▼'),      \() stat_sig == FALSE ),    what = c(      change_stat_sig_increase,      change_stat_sig_decrease,      change_no_stat_sig))   add_text(slide_obj = on_slide_number(2),          text = "▲5.4%", # Text to style          position = slide_position_3, # Where to put it          text_style = change_style, # How to style it          stat_sig = TRUE) # Any additional data we want our selection functions to have access to

title: Text Styling and Style Rules
text: styling system r2slides allows define formatting rules apply consistently across slides. Selection functions evaluated data-aware environment, can target whole strings substrings, can incorporate arbitrary logic. practice, makes straightforward : Encode statistical meaning visually Highlight structured labels Apply brand-consistent typography Build reusable formatting libraries pattern remains : Define styles text_style() Use + compose complex styles. allows define simple styles build complex ones without repeating . example, might bold style, small-text style. approach seem familiar anyone used tailwind CSS . Attach logic style_rule() Pass context explicitly add_text() add_text_multi()

title: Google Slides/Sheets creation from R

title: Google Slides/Sheets creation from R
code: chart_data |>   write_gs("Seahawks vs Dolphins Points Per Game") |>   get_chart_id() |>   add_linked_chart(     on_slide_number(       4     ),     in_top_left()   ) |>   add_text_multi(     month_change,     position_base = in_top_left(),     position = c(       seahawks_change_mm,       dolphins_change_mm     ),     text_style = change_style,     stat_sig = attr(month_change, 'stat_sig')   ) |>   add_text_multi(     moe_text,     position_base = in_top_left(),     position = c(       seahawks_point_estimate,       dolphins_point_estimate     ),     text_style = point_estimate_style   ) |>   add_text_multi(     year_change,     position_base = in_top_left(),     position = c(       seahawks_change_yy,       dolphins_change_yy     ),     text_style = change_style,     stat_sig = attr(year_change, 'stat_sig')   )

what: Add image to a Google Slides presentation — add_image
title: Add image to a Google Slides presentation — add_image
text: Inserts image onto slide. Accepts ggplot object, local file path, publicly accessible URL. given ggplot local file, image temporarily uploaded Google Drive, inserted slide, deleted Drive. image permanently embedded presentation insertion.

title: Add image to a Google Slides presentation — add_image
code: add_image(   slide_obj,   image,   position,   fit = c("fill", "natural"),   dpi = NULL,   order = c("front", "back"),   replacement_strategy = get_replacement_strategy(),   match_fn = get_match_fn() )

title: Add image to a Google Slides presentation — add_image
text: slide_obj Google Slides slide object. image ggplot object, local file path, publicly accessible URL string. image specified, must one following formats: png jpg gif position object class r2slides::slide_position. fit Controls image sized slide. One : "fill" (default): image occupies exactly position bounding box, ignoring aspect ratio. ggplot objects, plot rendered directly target dimensions. local files, magick package required resize image upload. "natural": image placed true pixel dimensions (converted inches using dpi), anchored top-left corner position. image may overflow bounding box. Requires magick package. dpi Numeric. used fit = "natural". DPI use converting pixels inches. NULL auto-detects file metadata, falling back 96. order Optional. One "front" "back". Controls Z-order created element. Default: "front". replacement_strategy One "add" (default), "replace", "skip". Overrides package-wide default set set_replacement_strategy(). "add" always creates new element. "replace" deletes matching element creates fresh one. "skip" leaves matching element untouched suppresses creation. match_fn function identifies existing slide element matching new one. Must signature function(new_spec, existing_elements) -> character(1) | NULL. See match_by_type_and_position() details built-implementation. Overrides package-wide default set set_match_fn().

title: Add image to a Google Slides presentation — add_image
text: Google Slides slide object (invisibly).

title: Add image to a Google Slides presentation — add_image
code: if(FALSE) { plot <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = cyl, y = hp)) +            geom_point()    on_slide_number(4) |>     add_image(plot, position = in_top_left()) }

what: Add a linked chart form Google Sheets — add_linked_chart
title: Add a linked chart form Google Sheets — add_linked_chart
text: add_linked_chart() takes existing Google Sheets chart embeds Google Slides presentation. default, linked, updates made chart also update presentation.

title: Add a linked chart form Google Sheets — add_linked_chart
code: add_linked_chart(   chart_obj,   slide_obj,   position,   linked = TRUE,   order = c("front", "back"),   replacement_strategy = get_replacement_strategy(),   match_fn = get_match_fn() )

title: Add a linked chart form Google Sheets — add_linked_chart
text: chart_obj Google Sheets chart object slide_obj Google Slides slide object position object class r2slides::slide_position linked Optional. logical indicating whether chart linked. TRUE (default): chart linked updates made Google Sheet also appear presentation. FALSE: Inserts image chart currently exists. automatically update. order Optional. One "front" "back". Controls Z-order created chart element. Default: "front". replacement_strategy One "add" (default), "replace", "skip". Overrides package-wide default set set_replacement_strategy(). "add" always creates new element. "replace" deletes matching element creates fresh one. "skip" leaves matching element untouched suppresses creation. match_fn function identifies existing slide element matching new one. Must signature function(new_spec, existing_elements) -> character(1) | NULL. See match_by_type_and_position() details built-implementation. Overrides package-wide default set set_match_fn().

title: Add a linked chart form Google Sheets — add_linked_chart
text: Google Slides slide object

title: Add a linked chart form Google Sheets — add_linked_chart
text: add_linked_chart() puts inputed chart exact area supplied slides_position object. means chart may re-sized.

title: Add a linked chart form Google Sheets — add_linked_chart
code: if(FALSE) { # Assumes that there is a chart already made on the references sheet  chart_data |>    write_gs("Seahawks vs Dolphins Points Per Game") |>    get_chart_id() |>    add_linked_chart(      on_slide_number(        4      ),      in_top_left()   ) }

title: Add a table to a Google Slides slide — add_table
text: slide_obj r2slides::slide object. table r2slides_table, object as_r2slides_table() method exists (e.g. flextable). position slide_position describing place table. order One "front" "back". Controls Z-order created element. Default: "front". table_id Optional character string. Object ID assign new table. Auto-generated NULL. debug Logical. TRUE requests returned instead submitted API. Default: FALSE. replacement_strategy One "add" (default), "replace", "skip". Overrides package-wide default set set_replacement_strategy(). "add" always creates new element. "replace" deletes matching element creates fresh one. "skip" leaves matching element untouched suppresses creation. match_fn function identifies existing slide element matching new one. Must signature function(new_spec, existing_elements) -> character(1) | NULL. See match_by_type_and_position() details built-implementation. Overrides package-wide default set set_match_fn().

what: Convert a table object to an r2slides_table — as_r2slides_table
title: Convert a table object to an r2slides_table — as_r2slides_table
text: as_r2slides_table() generic function converts table object (e.g. gt flextable) r2slides_table object suitable rendering Google Slides table element. Methods specific table classes return r2slides_table object.

title: Convert a table object to an r2slides_table — as_r2slides_table

title: Create a slide object — on_slide_id
text: id slide identifier. Can either: numeric slide ID string containing slide ID full Google Slides URL ps presentation object url URL pointing Google Slides slide n numeric slide ID slide slide object offset position integer - number slides reference slide. Can negative return slides reference slide text string search slide speaker notes. default exact = TRUE, notes must equal text exactly. Pass stringr modifier object (e.g. stringr::regex(...), stringr::fixed(...), stringr::coll(...) text use different matching strategy. exact Logical. TRUE (default) full notes string must equal text. FALSE, notes need contain text (matched Perl-compatible regular expression via stringr::regex(). on_multiple multiple slides match: error: (default) raises error listing matching slide numbers return: returns matches named list slide objects (names slide IDs)

title: Query Google API's — query

title: Query Google API's — query
code: query(   endpoint = "slides.presentations.batchUpdate",   params = list(),   body = NULL,   base = NULL,   token = NULL,   debug = FALSE,   max_tries = 4L,   backoff_base = 3,   call = rlang::caller_env(),   ... )

title: Query Google API's — query
text: endpoint single string indicating API endpoint. params Optional. list parameters API request. body Optional. request body. base Optional. string indicating API base service name. (.e. 'slides', 'sheets') token Optional. OAuth2 token. default uses r2slides_token() find token. debug Optional. TRUE, return unexecuted request. FALSE, execute request.Default:FALSE`. max_tries Optional. Maximum number attempts giving . Default: 4. Set 1 disable retrying. backoff_base Optional. Base (seconds) truncated exponential backoff. Default: 3. Wait time per attempt min(backoff_base ^ attempt, 60) + runif(1, 0, 1) seconds. call Optional. Call environment used error messages. ... Additional arguments reserved future expansion.

title: Query Google API's — query
text: processed API response. error endpoint recognized required parameters missing malformed.

title: Relative Position — relative_position
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Create a base position base_pos <- slide_position(left = 1, top = 2, width = 5, height = 3)  # Move right by 0.5 inches using a function, set the maximum width at 5 new_pos <- relative_position(   base_pos,   top_transformation = identity,   left_transformation = function(x) x + 0.5,   width_transformation = \(x) min(x, 5),   height_transformation = identity )  # Set absolute position using scalars new_pos2 <- relative_position(   base_pos,   top_transformation = 3,   left_transformation = 2,   width_transformation = identity,   height_transformation = identity ) } # }

title: Replacement strategy parameters — replacement_strategy_params
text: Replacement strategy parameters

title: Replacement strategy parameters — replacement_strategy_params
text: replacement_strategy One "add" (default), "replace", "skip". Overrides package-wide default set set_replacement_strategy(). "add" always creates new element. "replace" deletes matching element creates fresh one. "skip" leaves matching element untouched suppresses creation. match_fn function identifies existing slide element matching new one. Must signature function(new_spec, existing_elements) -> character(1) | NULL. See match_by_type_and_position() details built-implementation. Overrides package-wide default set set_match_fn().

title: Set the replacement strategy — set_replacement_strategy

title: Set the replacement strategy — set_replacement_strategy
text: "add" (default): always create new element; match function never called. "replace": delete matched element, create fresh one. match found, new element created. "skip": leave matched element untouched skip creation. match found, new element created.

title: Slide Position Object — slide_position
text: top position inches top slide (top-left corner rotation) left position inches left slide (top-left corner rotation) width width object inches (intrinsic width, rotation) height height object inches (intrinsic height, rotation) rotation rotation angle degrees (default 0). Positive values rotate clockwise around object's center. convert_slide_size logical. Optional. Converts position size Google Slides PowerPoint (custom) size TRUE. slide_size_old Old Slide size specifications. Optional. Used conversion powerpoint google slides sizes. slide_size New Slide size specifications. Optional.

title: Slide Position Object — slide_position
text: rotation applied, object rotates around center point. top, left, width, height properties describe object's intrinsic dimensions rotation. rotation applied affine transformation using scaleX, scaleY, shearX, shearY computed properties. get axis-aligned bounding box contains rotated object, use bounding_box() function.

what: String helper functions for text positioning — string-helpers
title: String helper functions for text positioning — string-helpers
text: Helper functions extract position information text styling pattern matching. functions return position vectors can used styling rules format specific parts text strings.

title: String helper functions for text positioning — string-helpers
code: str_before(   text,   character,   include_boundary = FALSE,   missing = c("error", "none", "all") )  str_after(   text,   character,   include_boundary = FALSE,   missing = c("error", "none", "all") )  str_matches(text, pattern, missing = c("error", "none", "all"))

title: String helper functions for text positioning — string-helpers
text: text character string search character character string search (literal match) include_boundary logical indicating whether include position boundary character missing character string indicating handle missing positions. Options "error", "none", "". pattern regular expression pattern search

title: String helper functions for text positioning — string-helpers
text: str_before(): numeric vector length 2 containing start position (1) position first occurrence character str_after(): numeric vector length 2 containing position first occurrence character end string str_matches(): matrix start end positions first match

title: String helper functions for text positioning — string-helpers
code: # Extract positions before and after a dot str_before("hello.world", ".") #> [1] 1 5 str_after("hello.world", ".") #> [1]  7 11  # Find pattern matches str_matches("hello.world", "ello") #> [1] 2 5 str_matches("test123", "\\d+") #> [1] 5 7

title: Style Rule Object — style_rule
text: style rule object list text styles applied set selectors.

title: Style Rule Object — style_rule
code: style_rule(when = TRUE, what = NULL, default = NULL)

title: Style Rule Object — style_rule
text: list selection functions determine apply style. function return either TRUE/FALSE (apply whole text) vector START_INDEX, END_INDEX (apply specific part text). list text styles. style corresponds selector . number styles less number selectors, last style treated default style apply selectors match. default optional default text style apply selectors match.

title: Text styling properties — text_style
text: Text styling properties

title: Text styling properties — text_style
code: text_style(   bg_color = NULL,   text_color = NULL,   bold = NULL,   italic = NULL,   font_family = NULL,   font_size = NULL,   link = NULL,   baseline_offset = NULL,   small_caps = NULL,   strikethrough = NULL,   underline = NULL,   alignment = NULL,   line_spacing = NULL,   indent_start = NULL,   indent_end = NULL,   space_above = NULL,   space_below = NULL,   indent_first_line = NULL,   direction = NULL,   spacing_mode = NULL )

title: Text styling properties — text_style
text: bg_color Background color. Accepts hex string (e.g. "#FF0000"), named R color (e.g. "red"), RGB numeric vector c(r, g, b) values [0, 1], theme color string (e.g. "ACCENT1"). text_color Text (foreground) color. formats bg_color. bold logical indicating whether text bold. italic logical indicating whether text italic. font_family string specifying font family. font_size numeric specifying font size points. link string specifying link URL. baseline_offset numeric specifying baseline offset points. small_caps logical indicating whether text small caps. strikethrough logical indicating whether text strikethrough. underline logical indicating whether text underlined. alignment Paragraph horizontal alignment. One "ALIGNMENT_UNSPECIFIED", "START", "CENTER", "END", "JUSTIFIED". line_spacing Line spacing percentage normal, e.g. 100 single-spacing, 200 double-spacing. Must >= 0. indent_start Indent start side (left LTR), points. indent_end Indent end side (right LTR), points. space_above Extra space paragraph, points. space_below Extra space paragraph, points. indent_first_line First-line indent, points. direction Text direction. One "TEXT_DIRECTION_UNSPECIFIED", "LEFT_TO_RIGHT", "RIGHT_TO_LEFT". spacing_mode Spacing mode. One "SPACING_MODE_UNSPECIFIED", "NEVER_COLLAPSE", "COLLAPSE_LISTS".

title: Write data to a Google Sheet — write_gs
Version 0.0.9075
text: New register_spreadsheet(id) new_spreadsheet(title) functions create lean R6 spreadsheet class mirrors presentation workflow. active spreadsheet stored package environment retrieved get_active_spreadsheet(), write_gs() longer requires global ss object. write_gs() now accepts r2slides::spreadsheet object, googlesheets4::sheets_id, object accepted googlesheets4::as_sheets_id() via new spreadsheet argument (default: get_active_spreadsheet()). sht_id chart_id now S7 classes property access via @. old new_sht_id() new_chart_id() S3 constructors removed — use sht_id() chart_id() directly. is_sht_id() now uses S7 inheritance returns TRUE chart_id objects. create_spreadsheet_env_in_global() google_spreadsheet global environment pattern removed.

title: Version 0.0.9074
text: solid_color() now stores red/green/blue numeric components (hex round-trip) accepts inputs . theme_color() new exported class Google Slides theme color references (e.g. "ACCENT1"). extend common r2s_color base class carries optional alpha channel. old transparent_color() class removed — pass alpha directly solid_color() theme_color() instead. new visualize() generic renders color swatch theme name. Colors alpha set used text-style contexts now produce warning alpha ignored.

title: Version 0.0.9073
text: New lazy evaluation strategy: use set_evaluation_strategy("lazy") buffer API requests instead executing immediately, flush execute_requests(). get_evaluation_strategy() returns current setting. view_request_buffer() inspects queued requests clear_request_buffer() resets buffer. execute_requests(batch_all = TRUE) (default) merges pending batchUpdate requests presentation single API call.
